bibliographic_data.title: How to Wake Up Your Neighbors: Safe and Nearly Optimal Generic Energy Conservation in Radio Networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: Recent work [5, 6, 9] has shown that it is sometimes feasible to significantly reduce the energy usage of some radio-network algorithms by adaptively powering down the radio receiver when it is not needed. Although past work has focused on modifying specific network algorithms in this way, we now ask the question of whether this problem can be solved in a generic way, treating the algorithm as a kind of black box. We are able to answer this question in the affirmative, presenting a new general way to modify arbitrary radio-network algorithms in an attempt to save energy. At the expense of a small increase in the time complexity, we can provably reduce the energy usage to an extent that is provably nearly optimal within a certain class of general-purpose algorithms.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents a generic method for reducing energy consumption in radio-network algorithms by adaptively powering down the receiver, which can be applied to any algorithm with minimal increase in time complexity.
core_content.key_methodology: Hierarchical clustering based on additive Voronoi decomposition at multiple scales is used to create layered cluster decompositions that help in efficiently simulating a large class of algorithms while reducing energy usage.
outcomes: SAF Simulation (Sleep when Activity is Far) algorithm reduces the energy cost of breadth-first search from 2O(√log n) to polylog(n).
The method can be applied to various problems including broadcast, BFS, leader election, and approximate counting.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In large networks of tiny sensors equipped with radio transceivers, the energy used in communication can be a bottleneck cost. Although it has become standard practice to measure this cost in terms of the number of messages or bits sent, the cost of using the receiver to listen for messages is often comparable to, or even more than, the cost of transmission. This suggests that receivers should be turned off except when there is an actual message to receive. However, this is easier said than done! In general, effective economization of receiver use probably requires redesigning our communication protocols from the ground up. The paper presents a generic method for reducing energy consumption in radio-network algorithms by adaptively powering down the receiver, which can be applied to any algorithm with minimal increase in time complexity. Hierarchical clustering based on additive Voronoi decomposition at multiple scales is used to create layered cluster decompositions that help in efficiently simulating a large class of algorithms while reducing energy usage. The SAF Simulation (Sleep when Activity is Far) algorithm reduces the energy cost of breadth-first search from 2O(√log n) to polylog(n). This method can be applied to various problems including broadcast, BFS, leader election, and approximate counting.

nodes.label: Hierarchical clustering based on additive Voronoi decomposition
